elements.sent-2411-001: Large language models (LLMs) are capable of solving a wide range of tasks, yet they have struggled with reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-002: To address this, we propose Additional Logic Training (\ALT), which aims to enhance LLMs' reasoning capabilities by program-generated logical reasoning samples.
elements.sent-2411-003: We first establish principles for designing high-quality samples by integrating symbolic logic theory and previous empirical insights.
elements.sent-2411-004: Then, based on these principles, we construct a synthetic corpus named \PLDItalic \ (\PLDAbbr), comprising numerous samples of multi-step deduction with unknown facts, diverse reasoning rules, diverse linguistic expressions, and challenging distractors.
elements.sent-2411-005: Finally, we empirically show that \ALT \ on \PLDAbbr \ substantially enhances the reasoning capabilities of state-of-the-art LLMs, including \llamaThreeLargeBaseline.
elements.sent-2411-006: Improvements include gains of up to 30 points on logical reasoning benchmarks, up to 10 points on math and coding benchmarks, and 5 points on the benchmark suite BBH.
elements.sent-2411-007: Knowledge and reasoning have long been considered essential elements for achieving artificial intelligence \citep{Mccarthy1959ProgramsWC,weizenbaum1966eliza,winograd1971procedures,colmerauer1973prolog,shortliffe1976computer,elkan1993building .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Knowledge refers to facts about the world, e.g., ``objects with mass generate a gravitational field'' and ``the Earth has mass.'' Reasoning involves combining multiple facts according to specific rules to obtain new knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-009: For example, the new knowledge that ``the Earth generates a gravitational field'' can be derived from the aforementioned two facts.
elements.sent-2411-010: Recent observations suggest that LLMs can solve problems using memorized knowledge of similar samples seen during pre-training, but they cannot solve novel, unknown problems that require reasoning \citep{hodel2023response,dasgupta2023language,zhang2024careful .}
elements.sent-2411-011: For instance, LLMs can solve famous arithmetic problems as is but not when the numbers or names are changed \citep{razeghi2022impact,mirzadeh2024gsmsymbolicunderstandinglimitationsmathematical , and they can solve coding tests from past years before the ``knowledge cutoff'' but not from the present year \citep{melanie2023blog .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: This bias towards knowledge has been observed even in state-of-the-art LLMs such as GPT-4 \citep{liu2023evaluating,wu2023reasoning,dziri2023faith .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{figures/others/performance_comparison.70B.pdf} \caption{ The performance gains to \llamaThreeLargeBaseline \ by Additional Logic Training (\ALT) on the proposed synthetic corpus, \PLDAbbr \ (\PLDItalic). Each benchmark set, such as ``Logic'' and ``Math'', comprises various benchmarks in that domain. \Cref{tb:performance_aggregated,tb:performance_details} shows the details. \label{fig:performance_comparison} }
elements.sent-2411-013: LLMs' poor reasoning capabilities can stem from the lack of high-quality reasoning samples in the pre-training corpus, which primarily consists of human-written texts \citep{betz-etal-2021-critical,pmlr-v202-morishita23a .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Indeed, reasoning samples in human-written texts often exhibit low quality, as evidenced by fallacies and biases commonly found in online debates \citep{hansson2004fallacies,guiacsu2018logical,Cheng:2017ud .}
elements.sent-2411-015: This is unsurprising given that humans usually think reflexively rather than through rigid reasoning \citep{kahneman2011thinking,SunsteinHastie2015,Paglieri2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Thus, a straightforward strategy to improve LLMs' reasoning capabilities is to prepare many high-quality reasoning samples and train LLMs on them.
elements.sent-2411-017: We propose one such approach, Additional Logic Training (\ALT), which utilizes high-quality samples of logical reasoning, the most fundamental form of reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-018: To prepare such samples, we utilize synthetic generation \citep{clark2020transformers,betz-etal-2021-critical,tafjord-etal-2021-proofwriter,pmlr-v202-morishita23a , where computer programs generate deductive reasoning samples in which a given hypothesis is proven or disproven by combining given facts following rigid reasoning rules.}
elements.sent-2411-020: In synthetic generation, computer programs generate samples according to pre-designed patterns, so this design largely determines the quality of these samples by nature.
elements.sent-2411-021: Thus, we start by discussing what is the ideal design for synthetic logic samples, incorporating symbolic logic theory and empirical findings (\Cref{sec:design_principles ).}
elements.sent-2411-022: The essence of logical reasoning lies in its ability to handle unknown facts, unlike knowledge, which deals solely with established facts, such as commonsense facts; therefore, samples must cover reasoning with unknown facts.
elements.sent-2411-023: Samples must include both illogical and logical reasoning to enable LLMs to distinguish between them.
elements.sent-2411-024: The samples must cover various patterns regarding a comprehensive set of reasoning aspects, such as reasoning rules and linguistic expressions of logical statements.
elements.sent-2411-025: We summarize these discussions into design principles, which guide the design of synthetic logic samples.
elements.sent-2411-026: Finally, based on these principles, we construct a synthetic corpus named \PLDItalic (\PLDAbbr), comprising numerous samples of multi-step deduction with unknown facts, diverse reasoning rules, diverse linguistic expressions, and challenging distractors (\Cref{sec:PLD ).}
elements.sent-2411-027: We then empirically verify that \ALT \ can enhance LLMs' reasoning capabilities (\Cref{sec:experiments,sec:results_and_discussions_method ).}
elements.sent-2411-028: Using \numBenchmarks \ benchmarks covering diverse tasks, we observed that \ALT \ on \PLDAbbr \ substantially boosts state-of-the-art LLMs' reasoning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-029: Even \llamaThreeLargeBaseline, the largest LLM pre-trained on over 15 trillion tokens, shows substantial improvements with \ALT \ (\Cref{fig:performance_comparison ).}
elements.sent-2411-030: Among synthetic logic corpora with different sample designs, \PLDAbbr \ yielded the largest performance gains, validating our proposed design principles.
elements.sent-2411-031: Moreover, we discovered that employing a knowledge-forgetting prevention method during \ALT \ is critically important, as it likely prevents the LLM's knowledge of established facts from being displaced by the unknown facts included in synthetic logic corpora.
elements.sent-2411-032: Finally, we analyze which task-solving capabilities \ALT\ can enhance and why (\Cref{sec:results_and_discussions_tasks ).}
elements.sent-2411-033: We observed a substantial improvement of up to 30 points on logical reasoning tasks (\Cref{tb:which_task_logical_reasoning ).}
elements.sent-2411-034: Surprisingly, we also observed improvements in abductive reasoning tasks, which go beyond the synthetic logic corpora's original deductive reasoning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-035: Case analyses indicate that these improvements result from LLMs having acquired the fundamentals of the logic reflected in the design principles.
elements.sent-2411-036: We also observed improvements of up to 10 points on math and coding tasks, indicating the generalizability of the obtained reasoning capabilities (\Cref{tb:which_task_math,tb:which_task_coding ).}
elements.sent-2411-037: We also observed improvements of up to 6 points on natural language inference (NLI) tasks (\Cref{tb:which_task_NLI ).}
elements.sent-2411-038: Case analyses suggest that LLMs successfully integrated the commonsense knowledge they had originally acquired during pre-training with the logical reasoning capabilities newly acquired from \ALT.
elements.sent-2411-039: Improvements across various other tasks (\Cref{tb:which_task_others ) demonstrate the broad benefits of the obtained reasoning capabilities beyond standard reasoning tasks, though the modest improvements of up to 2 points indicate the need for future research on more effective application of these capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Our contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-041: We propose Additional Logic Training (\ALT) and empirically verify that it can enhance the reasoning capability of state-of-the-art LLMs across various sizes, from 7B to 70B.
elements.sent-2411-042: We establish systematic design principles for synthetic logic samples; then, we construct a synthetic corpus named \PLDItalic \ (\PLDAbbr), comprising numerous samples of multi-step deduction with unknown facts, diverse reasoning rules, diverse linguistic expressions, and challenging distractors.
elements.sent-2411-043: We empirically verify that \PLD \ indeed leads to the largest improvements among corpora with different sample designs.
elements.sent-2411-044: We demonstrate that LLMs enhanced by \ALT \ can solve not only the original logical reasoning tasks present in synthetic logic corpora but also other tasks, such as math and coding tasks, and notably NLI tasks, which require integrating knowledge and reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-045: This finding underscores the potential for advancing truly versatile AI possessing both knowledge and reasoning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-046: We release the corpus, code, and the trained model under a permissive license \footnote{ \url{https://github.com/hitachi-nlp/FLD .}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{figures/others/ALPT_overview-crop.pdf} \caption{ Our proposed Additional Logic Training (\ALT) aims to enhance LLMs' reasoning capabilities through training on many synthetically generated logical reasoning samples. Our sample generator (left) first generates a sample of multi-step deductive reasoning and then converts it into a deduction sample written in English (right). LLMs must generate \textbf{\colorRedLogicalSteps{logical steps}} to derive a given \textbf{\colorVioletHypothesis{hypothesis}} from provided \textbf{\colorBlueFacts{facts}}. The sample generator adheres to theoretically and empirically grounded design principles discussed in \Cref{sec:design_principles}. Refer to \Cref{appendix:fig:deduction_example} for a real sample. \label{fig:ALT_overview} }
elements.section-2411-002: How Should Synthetic Logic Samples Be Designed?
elements.sent-2411-047: In synthetic generation, computer programs generate samples according to pre-designed patterns, so this design largely determines the quality of the samples.
elements.sent-2411-048: While Previous studies have examined several designs \citep{clark2020transformers,betz-etal-2021-critical,tafjord-etal-2021-proofwriter,pmlr-v202-morishita23a , these designs were not systematically discussed, so they may not be the most effective ones.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Thus, we start by discussing how to optimally design synthetic logic samples.
elements.sent-2411-050: To this end, we consider symbolic logic theory as suggested by \citet{pmlr-v202-morishita23a and integrate empirical findings from previous studies.}
elements.sent-2411-051: First, we observe that the essence of logical reasoning, based solely on the logical relationships between facts, lies in its ability to handle unknown facts, unlike knowledge, which by definition deals solely with established facts (\Cref{sec:principle_unseen ).}
elements.sent-2411-052: Therefore, we argue that samples should cover reasoning with unknown facts to represent this essential aspect of logical reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-053: We also observe that logical reasoning involves various other aspects, such as illogical reasoning, reasoning rules, and linguistic expressions that represent logical statements (\crefrange{sec:principle_negatives {sec:principle_linguistic_diversity ).}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The samples should cover various patterns regarding these aspects to enable LLMs to solve various reasoning problems.
elements.sent-2411-055: We summarize these discussions into the following design principles, which guide the design of synthetic logic samples.
elements.sent-2411-056: We first explore the essence of logical reasoning that differentiates itself from knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-057: Consider the following logical step: \begin{equation} \begin{mathprooftree} \AxiomC{The Earth orbits the Sun.} \AxiomC{If the Earth orbits the sun, the Earth has four seasons.} \BinaryInfC{The Earth has four seasons.} \end{mathprooftree} \label{eq:argument:first}\end{equation} This step is valid because the conclusion is logically derived from the two premises.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\begin{mathprooftree} \AxiomC{The Earth orbits the Sun.} \AxiomC{If the Earth orbits the sun, the Earth has four seasons.} \BinaryInfC{The Earth has four seasons.} \end{mathprooftree} \label{eq:argument:first}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-058: Next, consider another logical step: \begin{equation} \begin{mathprooftree} \AxiomC{The Earth orbits the Sun.} \AxiomC{If the Earth orbits the sun, the Earth does not have four seasons.} \BinaryInfC{The Earth does not have four seasons.} \end{mathprooftree} \label{eq:argument:second}\end{equation} The second premise and consequently, the conclusion, is factually wrong.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\begin{mathprooftree} \AxiomC{The Earth orbits the Sun.} \AxiomC{If the Earth orbits the sun, the Earth does not have four seasons.} \BinaryInfC{The Earth does not have four seasons.} \end{mathprooftree} \label{eq:argument:second}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-059: Nevertheless, if the premise was hypothetically correct, the conclusion could be logically derived.
elements.sent-2411-060: Therefore, step \Cref{eq:argument:second is also logically valid.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Finally: \begin{equation} \begin{mathprooftree} \AxiomC{1. A Foo star exists.} \AxiomC{2. If a Foo star exists, a Bar star also exists.} \BinaryInfC{A Bar star exists.} \end{mathprooftree} \label{eq:argument:third}\end{equation} ``Foo star'' and ``Bar star'' are unknowns; nonetheless, we can still determine that step \Cref{eq:argument:third is logically valid.}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\begin{mathprooftree} \AxiomC{1. A Foo star exists.} \AxiomC{2. If a Foo star exists, a Bar star also exists.} \BinaryInfC{A Bar star exists.} \end{mathprooftree} \label{eq:argument:third}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-062: Steps \Crefrange{eq:argument:first {eq:argument:third above can be abstracted into a deduction rule, i.e., modus ponens, using symbols: \begin{equation} \begin{mathprooftree} \AxiomC{$\mathcal{F}$} \AxiomC{$\mathcal{F} \rightarrow \mathcal{G}$} \RightLabel{\stackanchor{ modus ponens}{ }} \BinaryInfC{$\mathcal{G}$} \end{mathprooftree} \label{eq:argument:modus_ponens}\end{equation} As we have seen, the logical validity of a deduction rule depends solely on whether the conclusion is logically derived from the premises, not on the factual correctness of the contents of $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Therefore, the contents of $\mathcal{F$ and $\mathcal{G}$ can be arbitrary.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Now, we consider what kind of samples would be needed to teach the deduction rule \Cref{eq:argument:modus_ponens to LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-065: We assume a task to generate the conclusion given the premises as prompt inputs.
elements.sent-2411-066: If the learner were human, they would be able to infer the underlying deduction rule \Cref{eq:argument:modus_ponens by observing samples such as \Crefrange{eq:argument:first {eq:argument:second .}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: As a result, they would become able to solve the unknown problem \Cref{eq:argument:third .}
elements.sent-2411-068: However, from a purely inductive perspective, samples \Crefrange{eq:argument:first {eq:argument:second cannot simply be generalized to the deduction rule \cref{eq:argument:modus_ponens .}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: This is because the samples \Crefrange{eq:argument:first {eq:argument:second themselves do not contain the information that the contents of $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ are arbitrary.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: In fact, one could generalize samples \Crefrange{eq:argument:first {eq:argument:second to other rules; for example, the conclusion $\mathcal{G}$ can be derived if $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{F \rightarrow \mathcal{G}$ are given as premises and $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ include 'Earth' as their contents.}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Innumerable such deduction rules can be inductively inferred from the given samples.
elements.sent-2411-072: In other words, induction has arbitrariness \citep{hume1748enquiry,goodman1954fact,quine1969epistemology .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Humans prefer simpler rules \citep{russel1946,wittgenstein1922tractatus , so they boldly induce up to the deduction rule \Cref{eq:argument:modus_ponens .}}
elements.sent-2411-074: However, it is unclear how purely inductive learners such as LLMs, which extract only what can be inferred from samples without prior preferences, induce up to \Cref{eq:argument:modus_ponens .}
elements.sent-2411-075: For example, if only specific contents such as ``Alice is kind'' and ``Bob is smart'' are assigned to $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ in training samples, an LLM could develop into a machine that generates the conclusion $\mathcal{G}$ only when the input contains the specific contents.
elements.sent-2411-076: In order for LLMs to accurately induce that $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ are indeed arbitrary: Design Principle 1 (Reasoning with Unknown Facts).
elements.sent-2411-077: Prepare many samples assigning arbitrary contents to $\mathcal{F$ and $\mathcal{G}$.}
elements.sent-2411-078: They will make LLMs accurately induce $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ are indeed arbitrary, ultimately enabling them to reason with unknown facts.

elements.sent-2411-080: Suppose that we give this LLM a problem such as: \begin{equation} \begin{mathprooftree} \AxiomC{$\mathcal{F}$} \AxiomC{$(\mathcal{F} \land \mathcal{G}) \rightarrow \mathcal{H}$} \BinaryInfC{$??$} \end{mathprooftree} \label{eq:argument:and_modus_ponens_collapsed} \end{equation} Since the premises are insufficient for logically deducting the conclusion, outputting nothing is the correct answer.

elements.sent-2411-085: These samples will make LLMs induce that conclusions cannot be derived from insufficient premises.

elements.sent-2411-087: Since there are infinitely many possible logical formulas that can appear as premises and conclusions, there are infinitely many deduction rules.
elements.sent-2411-088: Providing LLMs with these infinite deduction rules is obviously intractable.
elements.sent-2411-089: Instead of directly providing these infinite deduction rules, we can take another approach.
elements.sent-2411-090: Consider multi-step deductive reasoning (\Cref{fig:ALT_overview left), where multiple deduction rules derive a conclusion.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Notice that the syllogism in \Cref{eq:argument:others can be expressed by multi-step deductive reasoning using more ``atomic'' deduction rules.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Indeed, there exists a set of atomic deduction rules called the axioms that satisfies the following:.
elements.sent-2411-093: In contrast to the axioms, the `compound' deduction rules, such as syllogism, contraposition, and De Morgan's laws, are called theorems.
elements.sent-2411-094: According to the completeness \Cref{theorem:completeness , if we can handle the axioms, we can effectively handle other deduction rules as well.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Indeed, \citet{pmlr-v202-morishita23a empirically verified that a language model trained on the axioms generalizes to handle other deduction rules more effectively than those trained on non-axiom deduction rules.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Samples should express multi-step deduction constructed from the axioms.
elements.sent-2411-098: They will effectively teach LLMs diverse deduction rules { \citep{pmlr-v202-morishita23a In multi-step deductive reasoning, the number of logical steps $s$ from premises to a conclusion can vary largely depending on the problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: Samples should include diverse numbers of logical steps $s$.
elements.sent-2411-101: Ideally, this would be sufficient, but empirical evidence has shown that LLMs struggle with constructing multi-step deductive reasoning with large steps $s$ \citep{gontier2020measuring,pmlr-v202-morishita23a .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Consequently, LLMs would not excel at handling theorems that require a large number of steps $s$ when expressed by the axioms.
elements.sent-2411-103: Therefore, as an additional countermeasure: Design Principle 3'' (Diverse Reasoning Rules).
elements.sent-2411-104: Samples should also include representative theorems, such as syllogism, contraposition, and De Morgan's laws.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Teaching Diverse Linguistic Expressions that Represent Logical Statements
elements.sent-2411-105: There are various linguistic structures for expressing the logical relationship $\mathcal{F \rightarrow \mathcal{G}$, such as ``If $\mathcal{F}$ then $\mathcal{G}$'', ``$\mathcal{F}$ leads to $\mathcal{G}$'', and ``$\mathcal{F}$ results in $\mathcal{G}$''.}
elements.sent-2411-106: If we only include specific expressions in the corpora, LLMs may only learn to react to these specific expressions, which has been observed in previous experiments \citep{zhang2022paradox,yuan2023can .}
elements.sent-2411-107: To prevent this, Design Principle 4 (Diverse Linguistic Expressions).
elements.sent-2411-108: Samples should include diverse linguistic expressions that represent logical statements.
elements.sent-2411-109: In this chapter, we have established the principles to guide the design of synthetic logic samples.
elements.sent-2411-110: Next, we construct a synthetic logic corpus based on these principles.

elements.section-2411-003: Creating a Synthetic Corpus based on Design Principles
elements.sent-2411-111: To prepare diverse samples reflecting the design principles 1 to 4 (DP1-4), we built a novel sample generator by extending the previous one by \citet{pmlr-v202-morishita23a and then generated the synthetic logic corpus named \PLDAbbr \ (\PLDItalic).}
elements.sent-2411-112: More specifically, our generator generates deduction samples through the following steps.
elements.sent-2411-113: First, the generator randomly generates a sample of multi-step deductive reasoning written in logical formulas, as shown on the left side of \Cref{fig:ALT_overview , where a conclusion is derived from premises using multiple \colorGreenDeductionRule{deduction rules (See \Cref{appendix:sec:FLD_proof_tree_generation for more details of this generation procedure).}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: At this time, the generator also generates `distractor' logical formulas, which express negative premises of DP2.
elements.sent-2411-115: Next, the generator converts each logical formula into English expressions.
elements.sent-2411-116: To achieve this, the generator first randomly selects a template from pre-defined options, such as ``If $\mathcal{F}$, then $\mathcal{G}$,'' ``$\mathcal{F}$ leads to $\mathcal{G}$,'' or ``$\mathcal{F}$ results in $\mathcal{G}$,'' for the logical formula ``$\mathcal{F \rightarrow \mathcal{G}$.'' It then assigns English content randomly constructed from a vocabulary, such as ``(that) a Foo star exists'' and ``(that) a Bar star exists,'' to each symbol, such as $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Finally, it converts the multi-step deduction into a deduction sample (right side of \Cref{fig:ALT_overview ) by using the premises as \colorBlueFacts{`facts' , the conclusion as \colorVioletHypothesis{`hypothesis' , and the intermediate logical steps as \colorRedLogicalSteps{`logical steps' .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: The deduction sample requires LLMs to generate \colorRedLogicalSteps{logical steps that derive a given \colorVioletHypothesis{hypothesis based on the given \colorBlueFacts{facts .}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: DP1: We assign $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ content randomly constructed from a vocabulary.
elements.sent-2411-120: While the existing corpora used small-sized vocabulary of up to 15k, we use a large vocabulary of around 100k words built from WordNet \citep{miller1995wordnet .}
elements.sent-2411-121: This will teach LLMs that $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ are truly arbitrary, ultimately enabling them to reason with unknown facts.
elements.sent-2411-122: DP2: The existing corpora used randomly generated logical formulas as distractors.
elements.sent-2411-123: In contrast, we implement adversarial distractors.
elements.sent-2411-124: For example, for a premise $\mathcal{F \land \mathcal{G}$, we use $\mathcal{F}$ with missing information (see \Cref{eq:argument:and_modus_ponens,eq:argument:and_modus_ponens_collapsed ), and for a premise $\mathcal{F \rightarrow \mathcal{H}$, we use $\mathcal{F \land \mathcal{G \rightarrow \mathcal{H}$ with missing information as distractors.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: These distractors teach LLMs precisely when a conclusion can and cannot be derived.
elements.sent-2411-126: As with previous corpora, we include a variable number of distractors in each sample, randomly chosen from a range of 0 to 20.
elements.sent-2411-127: DP3-3'': While the existing corpora used a small number of deduction rules of up to 13 (refer to Figure B.4 of \citet{pmlr-v202-morishita23a ), we include diverse deduction rules, encompassing the axioms and representative theorems, such as modus ponens, syllogisms, and contraposition, totaling about 50 rules.}
elements.sent-2411-128: We include samples with up to $s=8$ logical steps, following \citep{pmlr-v202-morishita23a .}
elements.sent-2411-129: DP4: We manually craft several more English templates per logical formulas than those used in FLD.
elements.sent-2411-130: Since the templates have a nested structure, they yield combinatorially more diverse English expressions.
elements.sent-2411-131: While counting the exact number of the resulting expressions is intractable, we observed at least dozens of expressions per logical formula, including minor variations.
elements.sent-2411-133: We briefly explain the experimental settings.
elements.sent-2411-135: Synthetic Logic Corpora: We examine the proposed \PLDAbbr \ and previous corpora (\Cref{tb:corpora ).}
elements.sent-2411-136: LLMs: We used the state-of-the-art LLM, LLaMA-3.1 (8B and 70B) \citep{llama3modelcard .}
elements.sent-2411-137: Training Settings: We trained the LLMs by a method similar to supervised fine-tuning; as illustrated in \Cref{fig:ALT_overview , we used the facts and hypothesis as inputs and logical steps and additional answer label (see \Cref{appendix:sec:answer_label ) as outputs.}}
elements.sent-2411-138: We excluded loss computation for the inputs to prevent LLMs from learning to generate unknown facts.
elements.sent-2411-139: We trained the LLMs for 1 epoch on 100k samples ($\sim$ 0.1B tokens) from the training split of each corpus, with a batch size of 256, resulting in 390 steps, with a linear warmup for 200 steps.
elements.sent-2411-140: We used the learning rate of 2e-05 for the 8B model and 3e-06 for the 70B model.
elements.sent-2411-142: Prevention of Knowledge Forgetting by Recall Adam Optimizer: Synthetic logic corpora include many samples with unknown facts, so training on them should cause LLMs to forget their knowledge of existing facts.
elements.sent-2411-143: To prevent this, we employed the Recall Adam optimizer \citep{recadam , which regularizes parameter updates to avoid deviating too far from the pre-training parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-144: Recall Adam stands out for LLM training for several reasons (see \Cref{appendix:sec:experiments_recadam for details).}
elements.sent-2411-146: The hyperparameters were: $\beta_1=0.9, \beta_2=0.999, \epsilon=10^{-6 , \text{fisher coefficient =4000$ for the 8B model and $2000$ for the 70B model.}}
elements.sent-2411-147: Benchmarks: We evaluated the trained LLMs on \numBenchmarks \ benchmarks shown in \Cref{appendix:tb:benchmarks using 5-shot in-context learning, except for BBH and AbuductionRules, which used 3-shot in-context learning.}
elements.sent-2411-148: These benchmarks cover a wide range of tasks and are prominent in LLM evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-149: Note that we excluded the synthetic logic corpora used for training, as training on them often leads to overfitting to their superficial and statistical cues \citep{zhang2022paradox,yuan2023can , failing to measure truly generalizable reasoning capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-150: We used lm-evaluation-harness \citep{eval-harness and bigcode-evaluation-harness \citep{bigcode-evaluation-harness for the implementation.}}
elements.section-2411-005: Can Additional Logic Training Enhance LLMs' Capabilities?
elements.sent-2411-151: Most LLMs trained with \ALT \ outperformed their counterparts without \ALT.
elements.sent-2411-152: Notably, \ALT \ yielded substantial gains of up to 10 points even for \llamaThreeLargeBaseline, the largest LLM pre-trained on over 15 trillion tokens.
elements.sent-2411-153: These results verify that \ALT \ can enhance the capabilities of state-of-the-art LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-154: Among the LLMs trained with \ALT, the one trained on \PLDAbbr \ (i.e., \llamaThreePLDALT) achieved the highest generalization performance across the benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-155: As seen, ablating any design principle almost always led to performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-156: These results demonstrate that the proposed design principles are critical to obtaining the maximum possible gain from \ALT, and each principle is indispensable.
elements.sent-2411-157: This behavior presumably occurred because the unknown facts included in synthetic logic corpora displaced the LLM's knowledge of existing facts.
elements.sent-2411-158: Therefore, knowledge-forgetting prevention is critically important for the success of \ALT.

elements.sent-2411-160: Surprisingly, we also observed improvements on abductive reasoning tasks, which go beyond the original deductive reasoning tasks in synthetic logic corpora.
elements.sent-2411-161: Abductive reasoning involves guessing the missing premises that caused the observed conclusion rather than deriving a conclusion from the premises.
elements.sent-2411-162: For example, from the observed conclusion, ``the window glass at home was broken and the room was ransacked,'' we guess the premise ``a burglar broke in.'' The improvements would be due to the fact that, while the surface form of abductive reasoning problems differs from that of deductive reasoning, they share the fundamentals of logic reflected in the design principles.
elements.sent-2411-163: Next, we conduct case analyses to see whether the LLM enhanced by \ALT \ acquired the abilities intended by the proposed design principles (DP1-4).
elements.sent-2411-164: The first problem is very simple, so it is surprising that \llamaThreeLargeBaseline \ failed to solve it, indicating the inherent difficulty of learning logical reasoning solely from pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-165: In contrast, \llamaThreePLDALTWoBold, which was additionally trained on \PLDAbbr, solved the problem correctly.
elements.sent-2411-166: The premises of the problem are randomly constructed to express unknown facts.
elements.sent-2411-167: Therefore, the result suggests that \llamaThreePLDALTWoBold \ acquired genuine logical reasoning ability, which can handle unknown facts (DP1).
elements.sent-2411-168: In the second problem, \llamaThreePLDALTWoBold \ correctly answered ``neutral'', indicating that it successfully learned that conclusions cannot be derived from insufficient facts (DP2).
elements.sent-2411-169: The third problem comes from the FOLIO benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-170: To solve this problem, LLMs must use syllogism at the first step as follows: ``All eels are fish, and no fish are plants.
elements.sent-2411-171: Therefore, all ells are not plants.'' \llamaThreePLDALTWoBold \ answered this problem correctly, suggesting that it successfully learned diverse deduction rules (DP3).
elements.sent-2411-172: FOLIO problems are created based on Wikipedia topics, describing them in more natural and realistic linguistic expressions than in other benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-173: As seen in the fourth problem, \llamaThreePLDALTWoBold \ understands such expressions, suggesting the effect of diverse expressions from DP4 and/or that LLMs can integrate their original linguistic ability with the newly acquired logical reasoning ability.
elements.sent-2411-174: The math improvements are reasonable, as understanding predicate logic is a prerequisite for solving mathematical problems.
elements.sent-2411-175: For coding, some recent studies have verified the opposite direction, namely, that training on coding data improves logical reasoning abilities \citep{jiang2024logicproimprovingcomplexlogical,ma2024at,uchiyama2024programminglanguagefeaturespretraining .}
elements.sent-2411-176: NLI is similar to deductive reasoning in assessing whether a premise supports or contradicts a hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-177: However, the main difference is that this judgment requires a rich set of commonsense knowledge beyond the given premise.
elements.sent-2411-178: Consider the fifth problem in \Cref{tb:case_study : by supplementing the given fact ``An Indian woman is dancing with her partner'' with the commonsense knowledge ``If someone is dancing, then he/she is moving.'', we can derive the hypothesis ``A woman is moving.'' The sixth problem is more challenging as we have to trace multiple logical steps while supplementing with sufficient commonsense knowledge as follows: ``a church choir sings at a church,'' ``baseball is often played at a baseball field,'' ``a person cannot be in two or more places at the same time,'' ``therefore, a church choir cannot sing for baseball.'' Since synthetic logic corpora only contain unknown facts, LLMs cannot acquire new knowledge from them.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Therefore, the commonsense knowledge used to solve the above problems must have been acquired by the LLMs from pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-180: This suggests that LLMs can integrate their original knowledge with the logical reasoning capabilities newly acquired from \ALT \ to solve problems.

elements.sent-2411-181: Improvements across various other tasks (\Cref{tb:which_task_others ) demonstrate the broad benefits of the obtained reasoning capabilities beyond standard reasoning tasks; though the improvements were modest at up to 2 percentage points, which may be due to the following reasons.}
elements.sent-2411-182: First, these benchmarks include problems that purely test knowledge, such as the first one in \Cref{tb:case_study_negatives .}
elements.sent-2411-183: Since \ALT \ does not aim to provide new knowledge, the ability to solve such problems does not improve by nature.
elements.sent-2411-184: Next, some problems may require knowledge that is too advanced for LLMs, so potential improvements by the enhanced reasoning capabilities may be bottlenecked.
elements.sent-2411-185: For example, the second problem does involve reasoning but requires sufficient quantum mechanics knowledge as a prerequisite.
elements.sent-2411-186: However, these knowledge-related issues should be solved by improving the quantity and quality of pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-187: Finally, LLMs may not be able to fully utilize the potential of enhanced reasoning capabilities for problems that require complex procedures.
elements.sent-2411-188: To solve the third problem, LLMs first must attempt reasoning related to each choice as follows: ``To build homes in an aquatic environment, one needs to maintain body heat and insulation despite being frequently submerged in cold water.
elements.sent-2411-189: Therefore, the waterproof fur of (A) is essential'', and ``To build \dots, one must gather and process natural materials like wood.
elements.sent-2411-190: Large, sharp teeth of (C) are critical as they allow beavers to cut down trees and shape branches.'' Next, while reasoning traces on (A) to (D) all seem reasonable, LLMs must choose the single best answer, considering the subtle nuance of the question context, as follows: ``Since the question emphasizes the aquatic environment, the least related reasoning trace should be (C).'' This complex procedure contrasts with logical reasoning and NLI problems, where LLMs can directly obtain an answer from a single reasoning trace.
elements.sent-2411-191: Previous studies also observed that such procedure on multiple-choice QA problems are challenging for LLMs \citep{joshua2023mcq,chuijie2024mcq,wang2024answers .}
elements.sent-2411-192: Since \ALT \ alone does not teach LLMs such task-specific procedures, additional training on these procedures should be necessary to solve these problems.
elements.sent-2411-193: Towards versatile artificial intelligence with reasoning capabilities, we proposed Additional Logic Training on synthetic logic samples.
elements.sent-2411-194: We established systematic design principles well-grounded on symbolic logic theory and previous empirical findings.
elements.sent-2411-195: We constructed a corpus named \PLD\ (\PLDAbbr) based on the design principles.
elements.sent-2411-196: We empirically showed that \ALT \ on \PLDAbbr \ substantially enhances the capabilities of state-of-the-art LLMs.

elements.subsection-2411-009: Investigation of Reasoning Capabilities of LLMs
elements.sent-2411-200: Many studies examine LLMs' reasoning capabilities \citep{askell2020gpt,rae2021scaling,razeghi2022impact,liu2023evaluating,turpin2023language,lanham2023measuring,wu2023reasoning,hodel2023response,dziri2023faith,dasgupta2023language .}
elements.subsection-2411-010: Synthetic Logic Corpus for Training LLMs
elements.sent-2411-201: Later studies \citep{saha-etal-2020-prover,dalvi-etal-2021-explaining,tafjord-etal-2021-proofwriter,sanyal2022fairr showed that T5 can generate even the intermediate logical steps as well as the final answer.}
elements.sent-2411-202: PARARULE-Plus \citep{bao2022multi is the enhanced version of PARARULE \citep{clark2020transformers , a variation of RuleTaker, that includes more samples and more logical steps.}}
elements.sent-2411-203: RoBERTa \citep{liu-et-al-2019-roberta trained on PARARULE-Plus outperformed the models trained on RuleTaker.}
elements.sent-2411-204: Artificial Argument Corpus \citep{betz-etal-2021-critical includes single-step deductive reasoning samples constructed from hand-selected deduction rules useful for critical thinking.}
elements.sent-2411-205: They showed that the GPT-2 \citep{radford2019language trained on this corpus can generalize to solve NLI tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-206: However, at the same time, they found that the LM does not generalize well to solve more challenging reasoning tasks such as ARC \citep{habernal-etal-2018-argument and LogiQA \citep{ijcai2020p501 .}}
elements.sent-2411-207: FLD by \citet{pmlr-v202-morishita23a,morishita-etal-2024-jfld is the first synthetic logic corpus based on formal logic theory.}
elements.sent-2411-208: It includes multistep deductive reasoning samples constructed from the axioms of first-order predicate logic, which can express any deduction rule due to the completeness theorem.
elements.sent-2411-209: Due to this nature, T5 trained on FLD generalizes most effectively to other synthetic logic corpora, compared to models trained on other corpora.
elements.sent-2411-210: They found that LMs can solve this task when there are relatively few proof steps, but it is difficult for them to generalize to solve proof steps longer than those shown in training data.
elements.sent-2411-211: To this end, they scraped sentences from Wikipedia using a template-based method and paraphrased them.
elements.sent-2411-212: They showed that training on this corpus helps solve real-world deductive reasoning problems such as EntailmentBank \citep{dalvi-etal-2021-explaining .}
elements.sent-2411-213: They verified that this data can enhance numerical reasoning, logical reasoning, and multi-hop reasoning abilities.
elements.sent-2411-214: While these studies partly examined the effect of synthetic logic corpora, whether this approach is promising remains an open question.
elements.sent-2411-215: It has been unexplored whether the capabilities obtained from synthetic logic corpora generalizes to solve various tasks beyond the original tasks in these corpora.
elements.sent-2411-216: Additionally, the effect of these corpora has only been examined for small LMs trained on small pre-training corpora such as T5 and RoBERTa; it has been highly questionable whether they can still benefit state-of-the-art LLMs trained on a huge pre-training corpus.
elements.sent-2411-217: Furthermore, even if their benefits were verified, it remains unclear which design of synthetic logic samples yields the largest benefits due to the lack of systematic discussions on sample designs and empirical verification of these designs.
elements.sent-2411-218: We aimed to answer these questions in this paper and demonstrate the potential of synthetic logic corpora.
elements.subsection-2411-011: Distilling Reasoning Traces from Very Large LLMs
elements.sent-2411-219: Recent approaches \citep{ho2023large,magister-etal-2023-teaching,li2022explanations,li-etal-2023-symbolic,shridhar-etal-2023-distilling,wang-etal-2023-scott,mitra2023orca,liu-etal-2023-logicot,benallal2024cosmopedia,lu2024mathgenie utilize very large LLMs, such as GPT-4, to prepare synthetic reasoning datasets to train smaller LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-220: A typical procedure is as follows: (i) prepare existing reasoning problems, (ii) prompt large LLMs to generate reasoning traces to solve these problems using techniques such as chain-of-thought prompting \citep{wei2022chain , and (iii) train smaller LLMs on these reasoning traces.}
elements.sent-2411-221: The distillation approach and the synthetic logic corpora approach examined in this paper have specific advantages and disadvantages, as follows.
elements.sent-2411-222: The advantage of the distillation approach is its immediate practical effect, as it directly teaches LLMs solutions to various existing problems.
elements.sent-2411-223: The disadvantages could be that (i) it is non-trivial for specific solutions to specific problems to generalize to other problems, (ii) the number of training samples is limited to existing problems in nature, (iii) the correctness and faithfulness of the reasoning traces are not guaranteed; indeed, some studies \citep{turpin2023language,lanham2023measuring suggest that large LLMs do not always faithfully follow the ``reasoning traces'' they themselves generate, and (iv) it cannot enhance the very large LLMs themselves by nature.}
elements.sent-2411-224: The advantages of synthetic logic corpus approaches are that (i) since they teach the fundamentals of reasoning, such as deductive reasoning, they have the potential to generalize to various problems, (ii) they can generate an unlimited number of new samples, and (iii) the correctness of the reasoning traces is guaranteed by nature.
elements.sent-2411-225: The disadvantage of this approach is that, as it only teaches the basics of reasoning, additional training may be needed to solve more complex real-world problems, as suggested in \Cref{sec:which_task_others .}
elements.sent-2411-226: We hypothesize that integrating both approaches could be promising.
elements.sent-2411-227: That is, we first train LLMs using \ALT \ to make them understand the fundamentals of reasoning through high-quality samples and then train them using more realistic reasoning traces to solve complex real-world problems.
elements.sent-2411-228: We only used deductive reasoning samples for \ALT.
elements.sent-2411-229: Future work should examine other reasoning samples, e.g., abductive and inductive reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-230: We only examined the first-order predicate logic system.
elements.sent-2411-231: Future work should examine other logic systems, such as modal and linear logic.
elements.sent-2411-232: The ultimate goal of the direction of this study is to develop an AI capable of reasoning logically step by step.
elements.sent-2411-233: If AI can make a decision one logical step at a time, it would be highly explainable and transparent to users.
elements.sent-2411-234: Furthermore, the user would be able to trace the AI's errors.
elements.sent-2411-235: We believe that our study is a step towards such AI that will positively impact society.
elements.sent-2411-236: Below, We briefly explain our sample generator.
elements.sent-2411-237: Please refer to \citet{pmlr-v202-morishita23a for the details.}
elements.sent-2411-238: In addition to the logical steps, the samples of \PLDAbbr \ and previous corpora include answer labels (\Cref{appendix:fig:deduction_example ): ``proved'' indicating that the hypothesis can be proved by the logical steps, ``disproved'' indicating that the hypothesis can be disproved, and ``unknown'' indicating that the given facts are insufficient for either proving or disproving the hypothesis.}
elements.sent-2411-239: For samples with ``unknown'' labels, the logical steps are ``None.''.
elements.sent-2411-240: Our sample generator first randomly generates examples of multistep deduction by forward- and backward random deduction, using the deduction rules specified by a user.
elements.sent-2411-241: The forward random deduction is done as follows.
elements.sent-2411-242: The generator first chooses a deduction rule randomly and forms the initial tree where the root node is the conclusion of the chosen deduction rules and the child nodes are the premises of the chosen deduction rule.
elements.sent-2411-243: The generator next randomly chooses another deduction rule that can be ``jointed'' to the root note of the tree.
elements.sent-2411-244: A deduction rule can be jointed to the root node of a tree if one of the premises of that deduction rule can be identified with the root node.
elements.sent-2411-245: Then, the generator updates the tree by jointing this chosen deduction rule.
elements.sent-2411-246: The generator continues this step multiple times until the tree achieves the required depth.
elements.sent-2411-247: The backward random deduction is done as follows.
elements.sent-2411-248: For each step, the generator randomly chooses a leaf node of the tree.
elements.sent-2411-249: Then, the generator randomly chooses a deduction rule that can be jointed to the leaf node.
elements.sent-2411-250: Here, a deduction rule can be jointed to the leaf node if the deduction rule's conclusion can be identified with the leaf node.
elements.sent-2411-251: The generator continues this step multiple times until the complexity of branches achieves the required level.

elements.sent-2411-252: We prepared linguistic templates for each logical formula, exemplified as follows: \begin{alignSmall \langle(A \land B) \rightarrow C\rangle: \ \text{If \ \langle(A \land B)\text{.predicate\_phrase}\rangle, \ \text{then \ \langle C\text{.predicate\_phrase}\rangle.}}}
elements.sent-2411-253: Expanding these templates beforehand is intractable due to the combinatorial explosion, so we expand these templates on the fly to randomly sample a single expression at a time.
elements.sent-2411-254: Estimating the exact number of expressions is intractable for the same reason.
elements.sent-2411-255: We manually crafted several additional English templates per logical formula (i.e., the left-hand sides of \Cref{appendix:eq:linguistic_templates ) compared to those used in FLD, which yield combinatorially more diverse English expressions.}
elements.sent-2411-256: We observed that at least dozens of expressions, including minor variations, are yielded for each formula.
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Prevention of Knowledge Forgetting by Recall Adam Optimizer
elements.sent-2411-257: We employed the Recall Adam (RecAdam) optimizer \citep{recadam , which regularizes parameter updates to prevent them from being too far from the pre-training parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-258: Recall Adam stands out for LLM training as it does not require access to the pre-training corpus, which is often inaccessible or too huge to handle, nor does it require changes to the model architecture, and it has a proven track record of usage in language models such as BERT.
elements.sent-2411-259: We show the average and standard deviations over five seeds.

elements.sent-2411-261: The checklist is designed to encourage best practices for responsible machine learning research, addressing issues of reproducibility, transparency, research ethics, and societal impact.
elements.sent-2411-262: Do not remove the checklist: {\bf The papers not including the checklist will be desk rejected.}
elements.sent-2411-263: The checklist should follow the references and precede the (optional) supplemental material.
elements.sent-2411-264: The checklist does NOT count towards the page limit.
elements.sent-2411-265: Please read the checklist principles carefully for information on how to answer these questions.
elements.sent-2411-266: For each question in the checklist:.
elements.sent-2411-268: Please provide a short (1–2 sentence) justification right after your answer (even for NA).
elements.sent-2411-269: They are visible to the reviewers, area chairs, senior area chairs, and ethics reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-270: You will be asked to also include it (after eventual revisions) with the final version of your paper, and its final version will be published with the paper.
elements.sent-2411-271: The reviewers of your paper will be asked to use the checklist as one of the factors in their evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-272: While "\answerYes{ " is generally preferable to "\answerNo{ ", it is perfectly acceptable to answer "\answerNo{ " provided a proper justification is given (e.g., "error bars are not reported because it would be too computationally expensive" or "we were unable to find the license for the dataset we used").}}}
elements.sent-2411-273: In general, answering "\answerNo{ " or "\answerNA{ " is not grounds for rejection.}}
elements.sent-2411-274: While the questions are phrased in a binary way, we acknowledge that the true answer is often more nuanced, so please just use your best judgment and write a justification to elaborate.
elements.sent-2411-275: All supporting evidence can appear either in the main paper or the supplemental material, provided in appendix.
elements.sent-2411-276: If you answer \answerYes{ to a question, in the justification please point to the section(s) where related material for the question can be found.. IMPORTANT, please:.}
elements.sent-2411-277: Question: Do the main claims made in the abstract and introduction accurately reflect the paper's contributions and scope?.
elements.sent-2411-278: Justification: Claims stated in \Cref{sec:introduction is supported by the experimental results in \Cref{sec:results_and_discussions_method,sec:results_and_discussions_tasks .}}
elements.sent-2411-279: The answer NA means that the abstract and introduction do not include the claims made in the paper.
elements.sent-2411-280: The abstract and/or introduction should clearly state the claims made, including the contributions made in the paper and important assumptions and limitations.
elements.sent-2411-281: A No or NA answer to this question will not be perceived well by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-282: The claims made should match theoretical and experimental results, and reflect how much the results can be expected to generalize to other settings.
elements.sent-2411-283: It is fine to include aspirational goals as motivation as long as it is clear that these goals are not attained by the paper.. \end{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-284: Question: Does the paper discuss the limitations of the work performed by the authors?.
elements.sent-2411-285: The answer NA means that the paper has no limitation while the answer No means that the paper has limitations, but those are not discussed in the paper.
elements.sent-2411-286: The authors are encouraged to create a separate "Limitations" section in their paper.
elements.sent-2411-287: The paper should point out any strong assumptions and how robust the results are to violations of these assumptions (e.g., independence assumptions, noiseless settings, model well-specification, asymptotic approximations only holding locally).
elements.sent-2411-288: The authors should reflect on how these assumptions might be violated in practice and what the implications would be.
elements.sent-2411-289: The authors should reflect on the scope of the claims made, e.g., if the approach was only tested on a few datasets or with a few runs.
elements.sent-2411-290: In general, empirical results often depend on implicit assumptions, which should be articulated.
elements.sent-2411-291: The authors should reflect on the factors that influence the performance of the approach.
elements.sent-2411-292: For example, a facial recognition algorithm may perform poorly when image resolution is low or images are taken in low lighting.
elements.sent-2411-293: Or a speech-to-text system might not be used reliably to provide closed captions for online lectures because it fails to handle technical jargon.
elements.sent-2411-294: The authors should discuss the computational efficiency of the proposed algorithms and how they scale with dataset size.
elements.sent-2411-295: If applicable, the authors should discuss possible limitations of their approach to address problems of privacy and fairness.
elements.sent-2411-296: While the authors might fear that complete honesty about limitations might be used by reviewers as grounds for rejection, a worse outcome might be that reviewers discover limitations that aren't acknowledged in the paper.
elements.sent-2411-297: The authors should use their best judgment and recognize that individual actions in favor of transparency play an important role in developing norms that preserve the integrity of the community.
elements.sent-2411-298: Reviewers will be specifically instructed to not penalize honesty concerning limitations.. \end{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-299: Question: For each theoretical result, does the paper provide the full set of assumptions and a complete (and correct) proof?.
elements.sent-2411-300: Justification: Our paper does not include theoretical results.
elements.sent-2411-301: The answer NA means that the paper does not include theoretical results.
elements.sent-2411-302: All the theorems, formulas, and proofs in the paper should be numbered and cross-referenced.
elements.sent-2411-303: All assumptions should be clearly stated or referenced in the statement of any theorems.
elements.sent-2411-304: The proofs can either appear in the main paper or the supplemental material, but if they appear in the supplemental material, the authors are encouraged to provide a short proof sketch to provide intuition.
elements.sent-2411-305: Inversely, any informal proof provided in the core of the paper should be complemented by formal proofs provided in appendix or supplemental material.
elements.sent-2411-306: Theorems and Lemmas that the proof relies upon should be properly referenced.. \end{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-307: Question: Does the paper fully disclose all the information needed to reproduce the main experimental results of the paper to the extent that it affects the main claims and/or conclusions of the paper (regardless of whether the code and data are provided or not)?.

elements.sent-2411-312: If the contribution is a dataset and/or model, the authors should describe the steps taken to make their results reproducible or verifiable.
elements.sent-2411-313: Depending on the contribution, reproducibility can be accomplished in various ways.
elements.sent-2411-314: For example, if the contribution is a novel architecture, describing the architecture fully might suffice, or if the contribution is a specific model and empirical evaluation, it may be necessary to either make it possible for others to replicate the model with the same dataset, or provide access to the model.. In general.
elements.sent-2411-315: While NeurIPS does not require releasing code, the conference does require all submissions to provide some reasonable avenue for reproducibility, which may depend on the nature of the contribution.. For example \begin{enumerate.}
elements.sent-2411-316: If the contribution is primarily a new algorithm, the paper should make it clear how to reproduce that algorithm.

elements.sent-2411-318: If the contribution is a new model (e.g., a large language model), then there should either be a way to access this model for reproducing the results or a way to reproduce the model (e.g., with an open-source dataset or instructions for how to construct the dataset).
elements.sent-2411-319: We recognize that reproducibility may be tricky in some cases, in which case authors are welcome to describe the particular way they provide for reproducibility.
elements.sent-2411-320: In the case of closed-source models, it may be that access to the model is limited in some way (e.g., to registered users), but it should be possible for other researchers to have some path to reproducing or verifying the results.
